did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach, specifically a VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning from ImageNet, to classify dermoscopic images for melanoma detection. The model was trained on four public skin lesion datasets (ISIC, 7-Point Criteria, PH2, and ISIC Challenge 2020) to distinguish between benign and malignant lesions, achieving an accuracy of 0.92. Grad-CAM visualization was also employed to interpret the model's focus on skin cancer areas.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning model (VGG16 with transfer learning) for melanoma detection. It details specific methodology including data acquisition from four public datasets, preprocessing, model architecture, hyperparameter tuning, and reports new experimental results (accuracy 0.92, recall 0.89, F1-score 0.91). Although published in a conference proceedings (KST 2024), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Detection on Dermoscopic Images Using Deep Learning Technique
journal: 2024 16th International Conference on Knowledge and Smart Technology (KST)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by developing a deep learning model using a VGG16 Convolutional Neural Network with transfer learning from ImageNet. The researchers aggregated four public skin lesion datasets (ISIC, 7-Point Criteria, PH2, and ISIC Challenge 2020) to create a balanced dataset of 13,986 dermoscopic images, which were split into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets. The model was optimized using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.001 and a batch size of 32, alongside data augmentation techniques such as random rotation and flipping. Experimental results demonstrated high performance, achieving an accuracy of 0.92, a recall of 0.89, and an F1-score of 0.91 on the testing set. Additionally, Grad-CAM visualization was employed to interpret the model's decisions by highlighting the specific skin cancer areas it focused on. The findings suggest that this deep learning approach holds promise for assisting healthcare professionals in diagnosing and screening skin cancers.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting malignant melanoma. The dataset includes 6,693 malignant images derived from the melanoma classes of ISIC Archive (6,701 images), 7-Point Criteria (252 images), PH2 (40 images), and the benign class of ISIC Challenge 2020 was used as the comparator. The final balanced dataset contained 6,693 malignant and 6,993 benign images.

The study used benign lesions as the negative class for binary classification. This included the benign class from ISIC Challenge 2020 (6,953 images randomly selected). The PH2 dataset's Common Nevi and Atypical Nevi were excluded from the final model training/testing set, with only the melanoma class from PH2 being used.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into benign or malignant (melanoma) classes.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'image classification' and 'binary classification between malignant melanoma class and benign class'. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using a VGG16 CNN with a sigmoid output layer for 2 nodes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score on a balanced dataset of 6,693 malignant and 6,993 benign images.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset collected by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) consisting of skin dermoscopic images. This study utilized only the malignant class from this dataset.

datasets_used.name: Combined Balanced Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset created by aggregating the selected subsets from the four public datasets above to resolve data imbalance. It consists of 13,986 dermoscopic images resized to 225x225 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the VGG16 model with data augmentation (random rotation, horizontal flip, vertical flip).
use.validation: Used for validation during model tuning.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (accuracy, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score by dividing results into true positive, true negative, false positive, and false negative groups.

To compare model performance across different combinations of optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSprop, Nadam), learning rates (0.01, 0.001, 0.0001), and batch sizes (16, 32, 64, 128) to identify the best performing configuration.
To evaluate model generalization by splitting the dataset into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, precision, and F1-score derived from a confusion matrix, and hyperparameters were selected via grid search based on these metrics without statistical significance testing.

Combined Balanced Dataset (Testing Set).notes: The best performing model configuration was VGG16 with Adam optimizer, batch size 32, and learning rate 0.001. The testing set consisted of 699 malignant and 699 benign images (total 1,398). The model achieved an accuracy of 0.92, recall of 0.89, and F1-score of 0.91. It correctly detected 625 malignant images (true positive) and 655 benign images (true negative), with 44 false positives and 74 false negatives. This configuration yielded the highest F1-score among all 48 tested hyperparameter combinations.

SGD.details: Evaluated with batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128 and learning rates 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001. Best F1-score was 0.90 (Batch 16, LR 0.01 or Batch 128, LR 0.01). Lowest performance was Batch 128, LR 0.0001 (Acc 0.77, Recall 0.63, F1 0.73).

RMSprop.details: Evaluated with batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128 and learning rates 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001. Best F1-score was 0.91 (Batch 64, LR 0.0001). Multiple configurations achieved 0.90 accuracy.

Adam.details: Evaluated with batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128 and learning rates 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001. Best F1-score was 0.91 (Batch 32, LR 0.001). This was the global best model. Other Adam configs achieved 0.90-0.91 accuracy.

Nadam.details: Evaluated with batch sizes 16, 32, 64, 128 and learning rates 0.01, 0.001, 0.0001. Notable anomaly: Batch 32/64 with LR 0.01 achieved 0.50 accuracy but 1.00 recall (likely due to predicting all as positive). Best balanced F1-score was 0.91 (Batch 16, LR 0.001 or 0.0001).

Hyperparameter Search Results (All 48 Co.notes: A total of 48 models were created and evaluated by combining 4 optimizers (SGD, Adam, RMSprop, Nadam), 4 learning rates (0.01, 0.001, 0.0001), and 4 batch sizes (16, 32, 64, 128). The Adam optimizer with batch size 32 and learning rate 0.001 provided the best overall performance.

architecture_details.layers: Five sets of convolutional layers, followed by three fully connected layers (original VGG16 structure)
architecture_details.modifications: Ripped off the original fully connected layer; frozen weights in the pre-trained layers; added two fully connected layers with ReLU activation function (128 nodes, then 32 nodes)
architecture_details.heads: Output layer with 2 nodes using sigmoid function for binary classification
architecture_details.classifier_head: Two fully connected layers (128 nodes, 32 nodes) with ReLU activation, followed by a 2-node output layer with sigmoid activation

training_details.optimizer: Adam (best performing among SGD, Adam, RMSprop, Nadam)

training_schedule.data_augmentation: Random rotation, horizontal flip, vertical flip

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: 138.4 million (original VGG16); specific count for modified model not reported

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing all images to a resolution of 225x225 pixels
Data augmentation on the training set including random rotation, horizontal flip, and vertical flip

Applied to the training set images. Specific probabilities were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed VGG16 deep learning model with transfer learning from ImageNet, optimized with the Adam optimizer (learning rate 0.001, batch size 32), achieved high performance in detecting malignant melanoma, with an accuracy of 0.92, recall of 0.89, and F1-score of 0.91 on the testing set.

Combining multiple public skin lesion datasets (ISIC Archive, 7-Point Criteria, PH2, and ISIC Challenge 2020) effectively resolved the issue of severely imbalanced data commonly found in previous melanoma detection studies.
Data augmentation techniques, including random rotation, horizontal flip, and vertical flip, were employed to increase the model's generalization capability.
Grad-CAM visualization confirmed that the model focuses on the specific skin lesion areas when deciding between benign and malignant classes, providing interpretability for healthcare professionals.

The study addresses the challenge of inadequate specialist health professionals and the difficulty of diagnosing skin lesions by offering a deep learning technique to assist in screening and diagnosing skin cancers.
important_remarks: The study aggregates four public datasets (ISIC Archive, 7-Point Criteria, PH2, ISIC Challenge 2020) to create a balanced dataset, but does not report external validation on a held-out, unseen dataset, limiting generalizability assessment.
The PH2 dataset's benign classes (Common Nevi and Atypical Nevi) were excluded; only the melanoma class was used, potentially introducing selection bias regarding benign lesion diversity.
The ISIC Challenge 2020 benign class was randomly sampled (6,953 images) rather than using the full available set, which may affect the representativeness of the benign class.
The paper acknowledges that similar skin lesions and artifacts (hair, moles, scars, markers) can negatively impact model performance, suggesting segmentation preprocessing could improve results.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed; hyperparameter selection was based solely on grid search metrics (accuracy, recall, F1-score).
The model was trained and evaluated on a single random split (80/10/10) without cross-validation, which may lead to optimistic or unstable performance estimates dependent on the specific split.
Data augmentation techniques (random rotation, horizontal/vertical flip) were applied, but specific probabilities or angles were not reported, limiting reproducibility.
The study was conducted using Google Colab with an A100 GPU, which may not reflect performance or computational constraints in typical clinical or resource-limited settings.
